articles.title: Google Is Betting Up to $40 Billion on Anthropic If It Hits Performance Targets
articles.summary: Google is committing at least $10 billion to Anthropic, the AI company behind the Claude models, with that figure potentially rising to $40 billion depending on whether Anthropic hits certain performance targets, according to a Bloomberg report. This makes Google one of the biggest backers of Anthropic, which is also backed by Amazon. A deal of this scale signals that the race to control foundational AI is getting enormously expensive.
articles.content:
Google is not messing around when it comes to AI. According to Bloomberg, Google will invest at least $10 billion in Anthropic, the company that makes the Claude family of AI models. But that number could climb all the way to $40 billion if Anthropic meets specific performance targets. That is a staggering amount of money to put behind a single AI company.

Anthropic was founded in 2021 by Dario Amodei, Daniela Amodei, and several other former OpenAI researchers. Claude, Anthropic's flagship AI assistant, has built a strong reputation for being thoughtful and safer than some competitors, which has made Anthropic a favourite among enterprise customers and researchers who care about AI safety. The company has positioned itself as the responsible player in a field that often moves fast and breaks things.

This is not Google's first investment in Anthropic, and it is not the only tech giant with skin in the game. Amazon has also poured billions into Anthropic, committing up to $4 billion in 2023 and expanding that commitment significantly since. So Anthropic now has two of the biggest technology companies on the planet competing to be its most important backer. That is a remarkable position to be in.

The $40 billion ceiling is the number everyone should be focusing on. It would make this one of the largest investments in any private AI company ever. It also tells you how seriously Google is taking the threat of falling behind in the AI race. Google has its own models, including the Gemini series, but betting this heavily on Anthropic suggests Google wants to cover every possible angle as AI becomes the most important technology of the decade.

For regular people, this matters because the money pouring into companies like Anthropic is what funds the next generation of AI tools you will actually use. More investment means faster development, more computing power, and bigger models. The question is whether all that money produces AI that genuinely helps people, or just accelerates a very expensive arms race.
keyPoints: Google is investing at least $10 billion in Anthropic, rising to $40 billion if performance targets are met
Anthropic makes the Claude AI models and was founded by former OpenAI researchers in 2021
Amazon has also made major investments in Anthropic, making two tech giants competing backers
The potential $40 billion total would be one of the largest private AI company investments ever
Google also develops its own Gemini AI models, making this a bet to cover multiple fronts

articles.title: Michael Jackson Biopic Michael Nears $90M Opening Despite Mixed Critical Reviews
articles.summary: The Michael Jackson biopic Michael is on course to land close to $90 million in its domestic opening weekend, making it the biggest music biopic debut ever. Audiences showed up in force even though critics were far from impressed, proving that fan devotion can overpower bad reviews. That number would shatter every record previously set by a music biopic at the box office.
articles.content:
Michael Jackson has always been box office gold, and the biopic simply named Michael is proving that all over again. As of Saturday 25 April 2026, tracking has the film heading toward a domestic opening weekend somewhere near $90 million. That would make Michael the highest-opening music biopic in history, and it is not even close.

For context, music biopics have historically been modest performers at the box office. Films like Bohemian Rhapsody, which covered Queen and Freddie Mercury, and Rocketman, the Elton John story, were considered major hits in the genre. Bohemian Rhapsody opened to around $51 million domestically back in 2018 and was seen as a benchmark. Michael is about to lap that number by a massive margin.

The remarkable part of this story is that critics have not been kind to Michael. Reviews have ranged from lukewarm to outright negative, with many pointing to the film's handling of the allegations and controversies that shadowed Jackson's later life. In most cases, that kind of critical response would hurt a film's opening. Here it simply did not matter. Audiences, clearly driven by love for Jackson's music and legacy, ignored the reviews entirely.

This opening tells you something real about the current state of movies. Fandom is more powerful than criticism right now. If the subject has a devoted enough audience, a rough review score becomes almost irrelevant. Michael is now a case study in that dynamic, and Hollywood will absolutely take notes.
keyPoints: Michael is on track for a nearly $90 million domestic opening weekend
That would set a new all-time record for music biopic openings, surpassing Bohemian Rhapsody
Critics were largely negative about the film but audiences showed up anyway
The result shows fan loyalty can override bad reviews for big cultural figures
Michael Jackson's enduring popularity is the real engine behind this record run

articles.title: Isomorphic Labs' AI-Designed Drugs Are Now Heading Into Human Trials
articles.summary: Isomorphic Labs, a spinoff from DeepMind, announced that its AI-designed drug candidates are moving into human clinical trials, marking a major step for the field of AI-generated medicine. Isomorphic Labs president Max Jaderberg made the announcement at WIRED Health in London, describing a 'broad and exciting pipeline of new medicines.' This is one of the first times AI-designed drugs have made it all the way from a computer model to actual human testing.
articles.content:
Isomorphic Labs just crossed a line that most AI drug companies have only talked about crossing. The DeepMind spinoff announced at WIRED Health in London that its AI-designed drug candidates are now moving into human trials. That is not a prototype stage or a lab result. That is a drug that was designed by an artificial intelligence system, cleared animal testing, and is now going into real human bodies to see if it works.

Isomorphic Labs president Max Jaderberg delivered the news, saying the company has built a 'broad and exciting pipeline of new medicines.' Isomorphic Labs was spun out of DeepMind, the AI research lab owned by Google, in 2021. DeepMind is best known in science circles for AlphaFold, a model that cracked one of biology's hardest problems by predicting how proteins fold into their three-dimensional shapes. Isomorphic Labs was built to turn that kind of AI capability into actual treatments.

The jump to human trials is a genuinely big deal. Drug development is brutally slow and expensive. Most candidates fail long before they ever reach a person. The fact that Isomorphic Labs has multiple candidates ready for human testing suggests the AI-first approach to drug discovery is producing real, viable results and not just impressive demos. For context, it typically takes over a decade and more than a billion dollars to bring a single drug to market through traditional methods.

What makes this especially interesting is what it could mean for the speed of medicine going forward. If AI can compress the early discovery and design phases, the time between identifying a disease target and having a drug ready to test could shrink dramatically. Isomorphic Labs is not the only player in this space, but reaching human trials puts it ahead of most of the pack.

The broader public might not feel the impact of this immediately, but the direction is clear. AI is moving out of the research lab and into the clinic. Whether these specific candidates succeed or fail in trials, the fact that they got here at all is a signal that AI-designed medicine is no longer a future possibility. It is happening right now.
keyPoints: Isomorphic Labs, a DeepMind spinoff, announced its AI-designed drugs are entering human clinical trials
President Max Jaderberg revealed the news at WIRED Health in London
Isomorphic Labs was founded in 2021 and builds on DeepMind's AlphaFold protein-folding research
Reaching human trials is rare and expensive, making this a significant milestone for AI-driven drug discovery
The company described a 'broad and exciting pipeline of new medicines' ready for testing
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Isomorphic Labs DeepMind drug discovery AI

articles.title: HandicapableSean Beat Super Mario 64 Using Only His Eyes After Two Years Away
articles.summary: Twitch streamer HandicapableSean, a disabled creator known for beating notoriously difficult games using only his chin, returned to the platform after a two-year absence and completed Super Mario 64 using just his eyes. This is a genuinely stunning achievement because Super Mario 64 requires precise three-dimensional movement and camera control that challenges even experienced players using standard controllers. HandicapableSean's return has already captured massive attention online and is being celebrated as one of the most remarkable gaming feats in recent memory.
articles.content:
HandicapableSean built his Twitch following by doing something most people would consider impossible. He plays and beats video games using unconventional physical controls, previously completing challenging titles using only his chin. His content sits at the intersection of gaming achievement and genuine human inspiration, and his community grew because people kept showing up to watch him do things that did not seem possible.

After two years away from Twitch, HandicapableSean came back with an even bigger challenge. He beat Super Mario 64 using only his eyes. Super Mario 64 is a 1996 Nintendo classic that is widely considered one of the most important games ever made, and it is not easy. Navigating its 3D worlds, collecting Power Stars, and managing the notoriously finicky camera requires quick, precise inputs across multiple axes. Doing any of that with eye-tracking technology is extraordinary.

Eye-tracking as a gaming input is a real and growing field, with accessibility researchers and hardware companies working to make games playable for people with severe physical disabilities. But completing a full game like Super Mario 64 this way, from start to finish, is not something many people have done. HandicapableSean did it after a two-year break, which makes it even more remarkable.

The response online has been huge. Clips of HandicapableSean's run are spreading across gaming communities and beyond, and his return has reignited conversations about accessibility in gaming and what the human body and mind can achieve when they are pushed. For a lot of people who grew up with Super Mario 64, watching someone beat it with their eyes is one of those moments that makes you put down whatever you are doing and just watch.
keyPoints: HandicapableSean, a disabled Twitch streamer, returned after two years away from the platform
He completed Super Mario 64 using only his eyes, with no traditional controller input
HandicapableSean previously became known for beating difficult games using only his chin
Super Mario 64 is a 1996 Nintendo classic requiring precise 3D movement and camera control
The achievement is being widely celebrated as a landmark moment in accessible gaming

articles.title: Trader Used a Hairdryer to Hack Weather Sensors and Win $34K on Polymarket
articles.summary: Police are investigating whether someone used a hairdryer to interfere with temperature sensors at Charles de Gaulle airport in Paris, causing false weather readings that lined up suspiciously well with winning bets placed on the crypto prediction platform Polymarket. The alleged scheme netted around $34,000 for whoever was behind it. It is one of the strangest and most creative financial frauds investigators have seen in the prediction market space.
articles.content:
Polymarket is a crypto-based prediction market where people bet real money on real-world outcomes, including things like election results, sports scores, and even weather events. Unlike a traditional bookmaker, Polymarket uses blockchain technology and pulls in data from real-world sources, called oracles, to settle bets automatically. The idea is that the system is objective and tamper-proof. Someone apparently decided to test that assumption with a hairdryer.

Here is what investigators believe happened. Temperature sensors at Charles de Gaulle airport in Paris began recording unusual readings. Those readings fed into Polymarket's data system. And the bets that were placed on those specific weather outcomes, before the weird readings appeared, turned out to be winners. The person or people behind those bets walked away with approximately $34,000. Police are now investigating whether the sensor readings were deliberately manipulated, and the leading theory is that someone physically held a heat source, possibly a hairdryer, close to the airport's weather sensors to artificially spike the temperature data.

It sounds almost absurdly simple for what amounts to a financial crime, but that is exactly what makes this story so fascinating. Polymarket's prediction markets are only as trustworthy as the real-world data they pull from. If you can manipulate the underlying data source cheaply and physically, the entire system breaks down. A hairdryer costs about twenty dollars. The return on that investment, if the allegations are correct, was $34,000.

This is not the first time prediction markets have faced questions about manipulation, but it may be the most concrete and physical example yet. Most concerns about market manipulation involve bots, wash trading, or insider information. Nobody expected the attack vector to be a piece of bathroom appliance held up to an airport thermometer.

For the broader world of crypto and decentralised finance, this is a genuine wake-up call. Polymarket and platforms like it need to think hard about how they verify the real-world data that settles their contracts. A blockchain can be perfectly secure while the physical sensor feeding it information is completely vulnerable. The investigation at Charles de Gaulle is ongoing, and no arrests have been announced yet, but the case has already sparked a serious conversation about the weakest links in prediction market infrastructure.
keyPoints: Police suspect someone used a hairdryer to manipulate weather sensors at Charles de Gaulle airport
The false temperature readings lined up with winning bets on the crypto platform Polymarket
The alleged scheme earned around $34,000 for whoever carried it out
Polymarket uses real-world data to automatically settle bets, making data accuracy critical
The case highlights how physical tampering can undermine digital financial systems

articles.title: David Lowery's Mother Mary Stars Anne Hathaway in a Film A24 Called Too Weird
articles.summary: Director David Lowery has made a film called Mother Mary starring Anne Hathaway as a pop star agonising over her comeback, and even A24, the studio famous for releasing strange and challenging films, initially thought it was too weird to make. Lowery opened up about the project in a new interview with Dazed, revealing how the film came together despite serious industry hesitation. If A24 had cold feet, you know this one goes somewhere genuinely unexpected.
articles.content:
David Lowery is not exactly known for playing it safe. The director behind A Ghost Story and The Green Knight has made a career out of films that sit in their own strange category, slow and haunting and hard to shake. But even by his standards, Mother Mary apparently raised eyebrows.

The film stars Anne Hathaway as a pop star who is deep in the anxiety of planning a major comeback. That premise alone sounds like it could go several directions, a glossy industry satire, a character study, a psychological spiral. From what Lowery describes, Mother Mary goes somewhere more singular than any of those. The fact that A24, the studio that put out Hereditary and Midsommar and Everything Everywhere All at Once, looked at this and thought it was too out there is a real signal. A24 has built its entire identity on backing the weird and the difficult. For them to hesitate means Lowery pushed past even that threshold.

Lowery discussed the film in a new Dazed interview published April 25, 2026, where he talked through the process of getting Mother Mary made and what drew him to the story. Hathaway's character is not just navigating the music industry. She is agonising over the details, the choices, the image, the meaning behind a comeback that clearly carries enormous personal weight. That interior pressure, the gap between the public performance and the private torment, sounds like classic Lowery territory.

Anne Hathaway has been on a quiet run of interesting choices lately, picking projects that lean into complexity rather than comfort. Casting her as a pop star wrestling with her own mythology feels like a smart fit for both her range and the film's themes. There is something inherently theatrical about pop stardom, all that constructed identity, which gives Lowery plenty to work with.

Mother Mary arrives at a moment when audiences are genuinely hungry for films that trust them to sit with discomfort. The A24 hesitation story is actually the best marketing the film could have. If the studio that made Lamb and The Witch thought this was too much, a certain kind of filmgoer is already buying a ticket.
keyPoints: Mother Mary is directed by David Lowery and stars Anne Hathaway as a pop star preparing a comeback
A24 initially passed on the film because they found it too weird, which is saying something given A24's track record
Lowery discussed the film in a new Dazed interview published April 25, 2026
The film focuses on Hathaway's character agonising over the details of her anticipated return to the spotlight
Lowery previously directed A Ghost Story and The Green Knight, both known for their unconventional tone

articles.title: Saudi Arabia Pulls $200 Million Offer from the Metropolitan Opera
articles.summary: Saudi Arabia has withdrawn an offer worth up to $200 million to the Metropolitan Opera in New York, a deal that would have been one of the largest single injections of cash in the institution's history. The Metropolitan Opera has been battling serious financial problems for years, making this withdrawal a genuinely painful blow. The collapse of the deal raises fresh questions about where one of the world's most famous opera houses goes from here.
articles.content:
The Metropolitan Opera just lost what could have been a $200 million lifeline. Saudi Arabia had put an offer on the table that would have sent up to that amount to the Met, and now that offer is gone. The withdrawal was reported on April 25, 2026, and it lands hard.

To understand why this hurts so much, you need to know the Met's recent history. The Metropolitan Opera has been in financial trouble for years. The pandemic nearly broke it, forcing cancellations across multiple seasons and brutal cuts to staff and productions. Even before COVID, the Met was running deficits and struggling to fill its enormous Lincoln Center hall on a consistent basis. A $200 million deal would not have solved everything, but it would have bought serious breathing room.

The Saudi offer was controversial from the start. Critics, including human rights groups, argued that accepting the money would amount to sportswashing, the practice of using cultural and sporting partnerships to improve a country's international image while deflecting scrutiny of its human rights record. Saudi Arabia has faced sustained criticism over issues including the killing of journalist Jamal Khashoggi in 2018 and restrictions on civil liberties. For many people inside and outside the arts world, the deal felt uncomfortable even if the money was desperately needed.

Now that Saudi Arabia has pulled the offer, the Metropolitan Opera is back to square one on this particular funding question. The Met has not publicly detailed its next steps. But with hundreds of millions of dollars in financial exposure and no easy replacement for a deal of this scale, the pressure on the Metropolitan Opera's leadership to find a new path forward is intense.
keyPoints: Saudi Arabia withdrew an offer of up to $200 million to the Metropolitan Opera
The Met has faced serious financial difficulties since before the pandemic
The deal was controversial because critics called it sportswashing
Saudi Arabia has faced international scrutiny over human rights issues including the killing of Jamal Khashoggi
The Metropolitan Opera now faces renewed financial pressure with no replacement deal announced
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Metropolitan Opera New York history finances

articles.title: Elon Musk Quietly Borrowed $500M From SpaceX as IPO Looms
articles.summary: Elon Musk took out roughly $500 million in low-interest loans from SpaceX to support his other business ventures, and a potential SpaceX IPO could force that arrangement into the public spotlight. The loans were structured to benefit Musk at terms that outside shareholders might not accept, raising serious governance questions. If SpaceX goes public, those sweetheart deals would face a level of scrutiny they have never had to survive before.
articles.content:
Elon Musk has been quietly borrowing from SpaceX for years. The total adds up to around $500 million in low-interest loans, and Musk used that money to help prop up his other companies and personal interests. Because SpaceX is still privately held, there has been almost no pressure to disclose the arrangement or justify the terms. That changes the moment SpaceX files for an IPO.

The loans were offered at interest rates far below what Musk would have paid on the open market. That kind of deal is only possible because Musk effectively controls SpaceX and sits on both sides of the table. A private company can let that slide. A public company cannot. The moment SpaceX has outside shareholders, those shareholders have legal standing to ask hard questions about where their money has been going.

SpaceX is one of the most valuable private companies on the planet, last valued somewhere north of $350 billion. An IPO would be a massive moment for the company and for investors who have been waiting years for a chance to buy in. But it would also mean auditors, regulators, and the general public getting a real look at how SpaceX's finances have actually worked under Musk's leadership.

This story matters beyond Musk himself. It is a case study in what happens when one person controls multiple massive companies at once. The conflicts of interest are real, and right now they are largely invisible. An IPO would not just be a business milestone for SpaceX. It would be the first time the full picture has had to be shown to anyone outside Musk's inner circle.
keyPoints: Elon Musk took approximately $500 million in low-interest loans from SpaceX to fund his other ventures
The loans were structured at favorable terms that are only possible because Musk controls SpaceX
A SpaceX IPO would force public disclosure of these financial arrangements
Outside shareholders would have legal standing to challenge deals that benefited Musk personally
SpaceX was last valued at over $350 billion, making a potential IPO enormous

articles.title: Noah Kahan Drops 'Last of the Bugs' Just 24 Hours After 'The Great Divide'
articles.summary: Noah Kahan surprise released an extended version of his fourth album 'The Great Divide', titled 'The Last of the Bugs', on April 25, 2026, adding four brand new songs to the record. The drop came less than 24 hours after the original album hit streaming platforms, catching fans completely off guard. It is the kind of bonus move that turns a good release week into a full cultural moment.
articles.content:
Noah Kahan did not even wait a full day. On April 25, 2026, less than 24 hours after dropping his highly anticipated fourth studio album 'The Great Divide', the folk-pop singer from Vermont quietly released an extended version called 'The Last of the Bugs'. Four extra songs, no warning, no countdown timer. Just there.

For anyone who has been following Kahan since his breakout run with 'Stick Season' in 2022, this feels very on brand. That album turned him from a cult favourite into a genuine mainstream force, landing him Grammy attention and a fanbase that treats his lyrics like diary entries they wrote themselves. 'The Great Divide' was already one of the most watched album releases of the spring cycle, and now 'The Last of the Bugs' gives listeners even more to dig into.

The move is smart for a few reasons. Dropping the extended version this fast keeps 'The Great Divide' at the top of social feeds for another full news cycle. Fans who already spent Friday night listening through the original now have a reason to go back Saturday morning. Streaming numbers tend to spike hard when artists do this, and with four new tracks, 'The Last of the Bugs' is not a deluxe edition in name only.

If you have not listened to 'The Great Divide' yet, today is a genuinely good day to start. You get the full original album plus four more songs, all in one sitting. Noah Kahan is clearly in a creative moment where he has more to say than a standard album can hold, and honestly, that is the best problem an artist can have.
keyPoints: Noah Kahan dropped 'The Last of the Bugs', an extended version of 'The Great Divide', less than 24 hours after the original release
The extended version adds four brand new songs to the album
The surprise drop on April 25, 2026 was unannounced, catching fans off guard
'The Great Divide' is Kahan's fourth studio album and one of the most anticipated folk-pop releases of 2026
Kahan broke through to mainstream audiences with 'Stick Season' in 2022
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Noah Kahan The Great Divide album

articles.title: Helena Bonham Carter Quits White Lotus After Role Misaligned On Set
articles.summary: Helena Bonham Carter has exited the upcoming season of The White Lotus, with a spokesperson confirming her character will be rewritten and recast. The role was created specifically for Bonham Carter by showrunner Mike White, but something did not click once filming actually began. It is a rare and candid admission from a production that the character simply was not working as written.
articles.content:
The White Lotus is one of the most talked-about shows on television right now. Created by Mike White for HBO, it follows a rotating cast of guests and staff at luxury resorts, with each season set in a new location. The show won a stack of Emmy Awards after its first two seasons and has become known for attracting major Hollywood talent to its ensemble casts.

Now comes a surprise bump in the road. Helena Bonham Carter, one of Britain's most recognisable and beloved actors, has left The White Lotus before her storyline could make it to screen. A spokesperson confirmed the news and was unusually direct about the reason. The statement said that the character Mike White created for Bonham Carter did not align once on set.

That is a pretty striking thing to say publicly. Most productions, when a high-profile actor departs, will quietly blame scheduling conflicts or creative differences without being specific. Here the implication is clear: the role that looked great on paper simply did not work in practice once Bonham Carter was actually performing it. Rather than push through and deliver something neither side was happy with, the decision was made to rewrite the character entirely and cast someone new.

For fans of Bonham Carter, this is disappointing news. She was one of the headline names attached to the new season and her particular energy, slightly eccentric, darkly funny, theatrically intense, felt like a natural match for the world of The White Lotus. Now someone else will step into whatever version of that role Mike White rebuilds.

The bigger story here is what this says about the creative process on prestige television. Even a show with the resources and reputation of The White Lotus can find itself in a situation where a role written for a specific actor does not survive contact with the camera. It happens in film all the time but we rarely hear about it this openly. Mike White and HBO deserve some credit for not pretending everything was fine.
keyPoints: Helena Bonham Carter has officially left The White Lotus
A spokesperson said her character did not align once filming began
The role was written specifically for Bonham Carter by creator Mike White
The character will be rewritten and recast rather than dropped entirely
The White Lotus is an HBO anthology series that has won multiple Emmy Awards
articles.wikiSearchQuery: The White Lotus TV series HBO

articles.title: Bootstrap Bio and Manhattan Genomics Collapse as Designer Baby Industry Implodes
articles.summary: Bootstrap Bio and Manhattan Genomics, two companies that were editing human embryos to prevent serious disease, have both shut down. Their collapse signals a dramatic unraveling of the commercial embryo-editing space, an industry that was already walking a legal and ethical tightrope. The shutdowns leave the field in turmoil just as the science was starting to look genuinely promising.
articles.content:
Bootstrap Bio and Manhattan Genomics are gone. Both companies were working on editing human embryos to stop serious inherited diseases before birth, and both have now closed their doors. The news broke on April 25, 2026, and it has sent shockwaves through the biotech world.

This is a big deal because these were not fringe operations. Bootstrap Bio and Manhattan Genomics were among the most visible companies trying to make embryo editing a real medical option, not just a science fiction premise. The idea was to identify dangerous genetic mutations in embryos before they were implanted, and fix them. Preventing conditions like cystic fibrosis or Huntington's disease before a child is even born.

So what went wrong? The embryo-editing space has always faced a brutal combination of regulatory pressure, ethical controversy, and investor nervousness. Ever since scientist He Jiankui was jailed in 2019 for creating the world's first gene-edited babies without proper oversight, the entire field has been operating under a cloud. Regulators in the US and elsewhere have kept strict limits on what companies can do with edited embryos, which makes it nearly impossible to run a clinical trial, let alone build a business.

With Bootstrap Bio and Manhattan Genomics both out of the picture, the question now is whether anyone will pick up where they left off, or whether the commercial embryo-editing dream is effectively over for this generation. The science has not failed. The business model has.
keyPoints: Bootstrap Bio and Manhattan Genomics have both shut down completely
Both companies were editing human embryos to prevent inherited diseases
Regulatory pressure and ethical controversy have plagued the embryo-editing space since the He Jiankui scandal in 2019
The closures do not mean the science failed, but the commercial path proved unworkable
The future of human embryo editing as a medical product is now deeply uncertain
articles.wikiSearchQuery: human embryo editing genetic engineering companies

articles.title: Ellen DeGeneres Returns as Dory in a New Pixar Finding Nemo Short
articles.summary: Disney and Pixar are bringing Ellen DeGeneres back to voice Dory in a brand new Finding Nemo short film, marking her first major Hollywood return since her talk show ended amid workplace controversy in 2022. This is a notable moment because DeGeneres largely stepped back from public life after The Ellen DeGeneres Show was cancelled following reports of a toxic workplace culture. The announcement puts her back in one of her most beloved and recognisable roles.
articles.content:
Ellen DeGeneres first voiced Dory in Pixar's Finding Nemo back in 2003, and the lovable, memory-challenged blue tang became one of the most iconic animated characters of that era. She returned for Finding Dory in 2016, which made over one billion dollars at the global box office. That is the franchise she is stepping back into now.

Pixar Animation is producing the new Finding Nemo short, and DeGeneres is confirmed to reprise her role as Dory. Details about the short's plot and release window are still limited, but the fact that Disney is bringing DeGeneres back at all is the real story here.

DeGeneres has kept a low profile since The Ellen DeGeneres Show wrapped in 2022. The show ended after a difficult period in which former staff and guests described a workplace that did not live up to its 'be kind' image. DeGeneres addressed the allegations publicly but the controversy significantly damaged her reputation. Returning as Dory, a character with no connection to any of that baggage, is arguably the softest possible re-entry into mainstream entertainment.

For fans of the Finding Nemo world, this is genuinely exciting news. Dory without DeGeneres would feel wrong, and Pixar clearly agrees. Whether audiences are ready to welcome DeGeneres back warmly is a different question, but a beloved animated short is probably the best vehicle she could have chosen to find out.
keyPoints: Ellen DeGeneres will reprise her role as Dory in a new Pixar Finding Nemo short film
This is DeGeneres's first major Hollywood return since The Ellen DeGeneres Show ended in 2022
The show was cancelled following widespread reports of a toxic workplace culture
Finding Dory, the 2016 sequel, earned over one billion dollars worldwide
Returning as a beloved animated character is a low-risk way for DeGeneres to re-enter public life

articles.title: Haruki Murakami's 16th Novel Features a Woman Lead After Years of Criticism
articles.summary: Haruki Murakami, one of the most celebrated novelists in the world, will publish The Tale of Kaho in July 2026, making it his first novel ever to feature a woman as the main character. This matters because Murakami has faced repeated criticism over the years for the way he portrays women in his fiction. The book will be his 16th novel.
articles.content:
Haruki Murakami is a global literary phenomenon. His novels, including Norwegian Wood, Kafka on the Shore, and the sprawling 1Q84, have sold tens of millions of copies worldwide and earned him a reputation as one of the finest storytellers alive. His work is known for its dreamlike atmosphere, lonely male protagonists, and a certain kind of melancholy that feels both deeply Japanese and universally human.

But that last part, the lonely male protagonist bit, is something critics have been poking at for years. Murakami's female characters have frequently been described as underdeveloped, existing largely to serve the emotional or romantic needs of the men at the centre of the story. It is a critique that has followed him throughout his career and one that has arguably held him back from even greater recognition, including the Nobel Prize in Literature, for which he is perennially tipped but has never won.

Now, with The Tale of Kaho coming out in July 2026, Murakami is doing something he has never done across 15 previous novels. He is putting a woman front and centre. Kaho is the lead character, and this is not a minor narrative shift. It is a structural one, the kind that signals a writer consciously reckoning with something about his own work.

Whether The Tale of Kaho will fully answer the criticism is something readers and critics will debate hotly when it lands. A writer's habits and instincts do not disappear overnight, and some will be watching closely to see whether Kaho feels like a fully realised person or a gesture toward one. The skeptics are already sharpening their pencils.

Still, the fact that Murakami is trying this at all is significant. He is in his late 70s, he has nothing left to prove commercially, and he is choosing to do something new. That takes a certain kind of courage for any artist, let alone one whose style is so instantly recognisable worldwide. July cannot come soon enough for the millions of readers who have followed him for decades and are ready to meet Kaho.
keyPoints: Haruki Murakami's new novel The Tale of Kaho publishes in July 2026
It is his first novel in 16 books to feature a woman as the main character
Murakami has faced long-running criticism for how he portrays women in his fiction
Murakami is frequently tipped for the Nobel Prize in Literature but has never won it
The book marks a significant creative shift for one of the world's best-selling authors

articles.title: Palantir Is Selling Chore Coats Now and That Should Worry You
articles.summary: Palantir, the controversial surveillance and data analytics company co-founded by Peter Thiel, has launched branded menswear merchandise including a 'chore coat', and it is part of a bigger Silicon Valley push into fashion. This matters because clothing is one of the most powerful tools a company can use to normalise its identity and make people feel proud to represent it. When a spy tech firm starts dressing you, it is worth asking what exactly you are signing up to advertise.
articles.content:
Palantir makes software used by the US military, intelligence agencies, and immigration enforcement. It is not a lifestyle brand. So when Palantir drops a chore coat as part of a merch line, the question is not really about fashion. It is about what Palantir is trying to become in the public imagination.

This is a well-worn Silicon Valley playbook at this point. Tech companies figured out years ago that culture is stickier than advertising. If you can get someone to wear your logo, you have done something an ad campaign could never do. You have made them a walking endorsement. Apple did it with minimalist aesthetics. Patagonia did it with environmental identity. Now companies like Palantir are doing it with a kind of rugged, utilitarian menswear vibe that borrows heavily from workwear and military surplus culture.

Dazed is calling this 'techno-fascist fashion', and that framing is deliberate. The chore coat is a garment with working-class roots, originally worn by labourers. When a defence contractor repackages that aesthetic and sells it back as merch, there is a real tension there. It launders the brand. It makes something associated with government surveillance feel approachable, even cool. That is the point.

The broader trend here is worth watching. Silicon Valley executives have been increasingly visible in menswear spaces, and tech money is flowing into clothing brands, fashion weeks, and style media. When the people building the surveillance infrastructure of the future also control how that infrastructure is styled and sold to the public, fashion stops being just fashion. It becomes a form of soft power. And a chore coat is a pretty affordable entry point into that game.
keyPoints: Palantir, a defence and surveillance tech company, has launched branded menswear including a chore coat
Silicon Valley is increasingly using fashion and lifestyle branding to shape public perception
The chore coat borrows working-class and military aesthetics to make Palantir feel approachable
Critics are calling this trend 'techno-fascist fashion' and flagging it as a normalisation strategy
When spy tech companies sell you their merch, you become a walking advertisement for their values

articles.title: Hulu's Love Overboard Is a Stanford Prison Experiment on a Superyacht
articles.summary: Hulu's new reality show Love Overboard, hosted by Gabby Windey, puts strangers together on a 280-foot superyacht in conditions designed to manufacture conflict, romance, and social pressure at the same time. The show opens with Windey greeting contestants with 'Ahoy there, perverts!' which tells you exactly what kind of energy Hulu is going for. The premise pushes the boundaries of what you can actually do to people on camera before it stops being entertainment and starts being a psychology experiment.
articles.content:
Gabby Windey's opening line on Love Overboard is 'Ahoy there, perverts!' and honestly that sets the tone perfectly. Windey, best known from The Bachelorette, is hosting Hulu's new reality show that takes a group of strangers, puts them on a 280-foot superyacht, and engineers the kind of social pressure that makes people act in ways they probably would not be proud of on land. The show is glossy, chaotic, and weirdly hard to look away from.

The Stanford Prison Experiment reference is not just a catchy comparison. The original Stanford Prison Experiment took place in 1971 when psychologist Philip Zimbardo randomly assigned college students to be either prisoners or guards in a simulated jail. It had to be shut down after six days because participants fully adopted their roles, with guards becoming cruel and prisoners showing signs of psychological breakdown. The experiment is now famous as a case study in how quickly people change their behaviour when they are put into artificial structures with defined power and social roles. Love Overboard is not a psychology study, but the design of the show is clearly built around the same basic idea: put people in a confined, high-stakes social environment and watch what happens.

The superyacht setting does a lot of work here. There is nowhere to go. Everyone is watching everyone else. Resources, space, and social status all feel scarce even on a luxury vessel. Producers know exactly what they are doing by choosing a setting that looks aspirational from the outside but functions like a pressure cooker on the inside. The 280-foot yacht is big enough to feel grand and small enough to make escape impossible.

What separates Love Overboard from standard reality TV drama is how consciously it seems to be engineering psychological stress rather than just hoping it happens organically. The show layers in romantic competition on top of social elimination on top of the claustrophobia of being at sea. That is a lot of overlapping pressure systems pointed at the same group of people.

The real question is whether this crosses a line. Reality TV has always played with manipulation, but there is a growing conversation about what duty of care producers actually owe to contestants. Love Overboard seems to be testing exactly how close to the edge you can get before audiences start asking uncomfortable questions. For now, it seems like Hulu is betting that a 280-foot yacht and Gabby Windey's charisma will keep viewers too entertained to ask those questions at all.
keyPoints: Love Overboard is a new Hulu reality show hosted by Gabby Windey set on a 280-foot superyacht
The show's design mirrors the social pressure mechanics of the 1971 Stanford Prison Experiment
Windey opens the show with 'Ahoy there, perverts!' signaling its deliberately provocative tone
The confined, high-stakes setting is engineered to manufacture conflict, romance, and psychological pressure simultaneously
The show raises questions about how far reality TV can push contestants before it becomes ethically problematic

articles.title: Looksmaxxing Gets Its Own Film Starring God of War's Sunny Suljic
articles.summary: The looksmaxxing trend, which has taken over TikTok and teenage culture, is now getting the Hollywood short film treatment with Sunny Suljic in the lead role. Suljic is best known for playing Atreus in the God of War video game series, making him a fitting face for a story rooted in youth and self-image. This is one of the clearest signs yet that looksmaxxing has crossed from internet subculture into mainstream pop culture.
articles.content:
Looksmaxxing is the obsessive pursuit of physical self-improvement, and it has been everywhere online for the past couple of years. Young people, mostly teenage boys, share tips on jaw exercises, skincare routines, and even more extreme measures to look more attractive. It started as a niche forum thing and exploded into a full-blown TikTok phenomenon, with millions of videos racking up billions of views.

Now it is getting its own short film, and the lead actor is Sunny Suljic. You might know Suljic from his role as Atreus, the son of Kratos in Sony's God of War games, which made him one of the most recognisable young faces in gaming. He also starred in Jonah Hill's 2018 skateboarding film Mid90s, so he has real experience playing kids caught up in intense subcultures.

The film leans directly into the world of looksmaxxing, exploring what it does to a young person's head when their entire focus becomes optimising their appearance. That is the part of this trend that has worried a lot of parents and mental health experts. The pressure to constantly improve your looks, often based on brutal online feedback, can get pretty dark pretty fast.

Having a film made about looksmaxxing is a genuine cultural milestone. When a niche internet trend gets a scripted story built around it, you know it has fully arrived. Casting Suljic, someone who already resonates with the exact demographic deep in this trend, is a smart move. This one is worth watching.
keyPoints: Looksmaxxing is a viral trend where mostly young men obsessively try to maximise their physical attractiveness
A new short film is being made based on the trend
Sunny Suljic, who plays Atreus in God of War, stars in the film
Suljic previously starred in Mid90s, another film about youth subculture
The film signals looksmaxxing has officially crossed from internet niche to mainstream culture

articles.title: Michael Stipe Debuts Solo Song "The Rest Of Ever" Live On Colbert
articles.summary: Michael Stipe appeared on The Late Show with Stephen Colbert on Thursday night and performed a brand new unreleased song called "The Rest Of Ever" live for the first time. It is a significant moment because Stipe, best known as the frontman of R.E.M., is finally working on his first ever solo album. The beard is also, apparently, quite something.
articles.content:
Michael Stipe has been one of rock music's most beloved voices for over four decades, first as the lead singer of R.E.M., the Athens, Georgia band that gave us "Losing My Religion" and "Everybody Hurts" before splitting up in 2011. Since then Stipe has been relatively quiet on the music front, which makes Thursday night's appearance on The Late Show with Stephen Colbert a genuinely exciting event.

Stipe sat down with Colbert for a candid interview and then stepped up to perform "The Rest Of Ever" live, marking the very first time the song has been played in front of an audience. The performance gave the world its first real taste of what a Michael Stipe solo record might sound and feel like, and by all accounts it landed well.

The big news wrapped around the performance is that Stipe confirmed he is working on his first solo album. That is not a small thing. Plenty of artists from legendary bands have gone solo over the years but Stipe has always felt like someone who did not need to prove anything outside of R.E.M., which makes the fact that he is doing it now all the more intriguing. Whatever direction "The Rest Of Ever" points toward, fans are paying close attention.

Stipe showed up to the Colbert taping with what Stereogum described as a sick beard, which feels fitting for an artist stepping back into the spotlight on his own terms, unhurried and very much himself. If "The Rest Of Ever" is any indication, the solo album is going to be worth the wait.
keyPoints: Michael Stipe performed new song "The Rest Of Ever" live for the first time on The Late Show with Stephen Colbert
Stipe confirmed he is working on his first ever solo album
Stipe was the frontman of R.E.M., who broke up in 2011 after more than 30 years together
This is one of Stipe's most significant public music appearances since R.E.M. disbanded

articles.title: Jim Furyk Named US Ryder Cup Captain Again for 2027 Ireland Showdown
articles.summary: Jim Furyk has been confirmed as the United States Ryder Cup captain for the 2027 competition, which will be held in Ireland. This is a return to the role for Furyk, who previously captained the US team at the 2018 Ryder Cup in France, where the Americans lost to Europe. Ireland hosting the event makes the 2027 edition a huge deal for European golf fans.
articles.content:
Jim Furyk is heading back to the captain's chair. The PGA of America has confirmed that Furyk will lead the United States Ryder Cup team in 2027, making him one of the few people to captain the US side more than once in the modern era.

The Ryder Cup, for anyone new to it, is the biggest team event in golf. It pits the United States against Europe every two years, and it produces some of the most electric moments in the sport. Fans are passionate, the atmosphere is loud, and national pride is fully on the line. Furyk first captained the US in 2018 at Le Golf National in France, where Europe won 17.5 to 10.5. That was a heavy loss, so Furyk will have unfinished business going into 2027.

The location makes this one extra special. The 2027 Ryder Cup will take place in Ireland, which means Europe will have an enormous home crowd advantage. Irish golf fans are famously passionate, and hosting the Ryder Cup on Irish soil for the first time is a massive moment for the country. The exact course has not yet been confirmed, but the atmosphere is already guaranteed to be electric.

Furyk is a well-respected figure in American golf and a World Golf Hall of Fame inductee. His experience as both a player and a previous captain means the US team will be in steady hands. But convincing a roaring Irish crowd that America is taking back the trophy is going to be one of sport's toughest challenges in 2027.
keyPoints: Jim Furyk is confirmed as US Ryder Cup captain for 2027
The 2027 Ryder Cup will be held in Ireland, a first for the country
Furyk previously captained the US at the 2018 Ryder Cup in France, which Europe won 17.5 to 10.5
Europe will have a significant home crowd advantage in Ireland
Furyk is a World Golf Hall of Fame inductee
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Jim Furyk golfer Ryder Cup captain
